issues.original: Every Python developer has lived through this moment: you run a script on your colleague's machine and it crashes because they have Python 3.8 while you wrote it on 3.11. Or worse, you [CODE] something globally and break a completely unrelated project. Python's environment story is powerful once you understand it, but the default experience is a minefield.
issues.fixed: Every Python developer has experienced this: you run a script on a colleague's machine, and it crashes because they have Python 3.8, while you wrote it on 3.11. Or worse, you [CODE] something globally and break an unrelated project. Python's environment management is powerful once you understand it, but the default experience is a minefield.

issues.original: This article walks through the entire toolchain from scratch. By the end, you'll have a reproducible, isolated, version-pinned setup that works the same way on every machine.
issues.fixed: This article walks through the entire toolchain from scratch. By the end, you'll have a reproducible, isolated, and version-pinned setup that works the same way on every machine.

issues.original: Most operating systems come with a system Python. On macOS, it was Python 2.7 (removed in Monterey). On Ubuntu 22.04, it's Python 3.10. This system Python is used by OS-level tools. Installing or upgrading packages in it can break your operating system.
issues.fixed: Most operating systems come with a system Python. On macOS, it was Python 2.7 (removed in Monterey). On Ubuntu 22.04, it's Python 3.10. This system Python is used by OS-level tools, and installing or upgrading packages in it can break your operating system.

issues.original:
The core problems:

| Problem | Example |
|---------|---------|
| System Python is outdated | Ubuntu 20.04 ships 3.8, you need 3.11 features |
| Multiple projects need different versions | Project A needs 3.9, Project B needs 3.12 |
| Global pip installs cause conflicts | Package X needs [CODE], Package Y pins [CODE] |
| Reproducibility fails | "Works on my machine" because versions differ |
| OS tools depend on system Python | [CODE] on Ubuntu uses system Python internally |
issues.fixed:
The core problems:

| Problem | Example |
|---------|---------|
| System Python is outdated | Ubuntu 20.04 ships with 3.8, but you need 3.11 features |
| Multiple projects need different versions | Project A needs 3.9, Project B needs 3.12 |
| Global pip installs cause conflicts | Package X needs [CODE], Package Y pins [CODE] |
| Reproducibility fails | "Works on my machine" because versions differ |
| OS tools depend on system Python | [CODE] on Ubuntu uses system Python internally |

issues.original:
The solution is a three-layer stack:

1. **pyenv** manages Python versions (install 3.9, 3.10, 3.11 side by side)
2. **venv** isolates per-project dependencies
3. **pip-tools** or **Poetry** pin exact versions for reproducibility
issues.fixed:
The solution is a three-layer stack:

1. **pyenv** manages Python versions (install 3.9, 3.10, and 3.11 side by side)
2. **venv** isolates per-project dependencies
3. **pip-tools** or **Poetry** pin exact versions for reproducibility

issues.original: pyenv intercepts the [CODE] command and redirects it to whichever version you have configured. It does this by inserting shims into your [CODE].
issues.fixed: pyenv intercepts the [CODE] command and redirects it to the version you have configured. It does this by inserting shims into your [CODE].

issues.original: Even with the right Python version, you still need dependency isolation. Without it, [CODE] puts packages into a shared location, and two projects needing different versions of the same package will conflict.
issues.fixed: Even with the right Python version, you still need dependency isolation. Without it, [CODE] puts packages in a shared location, causing conflicts between projects that need different versions of the same package.

issues.original: Never commit the virtual environment; it contains platform-specific binaries and is not portable.
issues.fixed: Never commit the virtual environment, as it contains platform-specific binaries and is not portable.

issues.original: With your venv activated, [CODE] installs packages into the isolated environment.
issues.fixed: With your venv activated, [CODE] installs packages into the isolated environment.

issues.original: Every project starts as a single file. You write [CODE], it works, you add features, and one day you realize you have 1,500 lines in one file with functions that call other functions that depend on globals defined 800 lines above. The code works, but nobody (including future you) can understand it.
issues.fixed: Every project starts as a single file. You write [CODE], it works, and you add features. One day, you realize you have 1,500 lines in one file, with functions calling each other and depending on globals defined 800 lines above. The code works, but nobody (including future you) can understand it.

issues.original: The jump from script to package is the first real engineering decision in a Python project. Get it right early, and testing, packaging, and deployment become easier. Get it wrong, and you'll spend weeks untangling circular imports.
issues.fixed: The jump from script to package is the first real engineering decision in a Python project. Get it right early, and testing, packaging, and deployment become easier. Get it wrong, and you'll spend weeks untangling circular imports.

issues.original: A single-file script is fine when: - The code is under 300 lines - There is one clear flow from top to bottom - You are the only person who will ever read it - It is a throwaway script, not a maintained tool
issues.fixed: A single-file script is fine when: - The code is under 300 lines - There is one clear flow from top to bottom - You are the only person who will ever read it - It is a throwaway script, not a maintained tool

issues.original: You need a package when: - Multiple people work on the code - You want to test individual components - You need to reuse functions across scripts - The code has distinct logical sections (config, data, logic, CLI) - You plan to distribute it (pip install)
issues.fixed: You need a package when: - Multiple people work on the code - You want to test individual components - You need to reuse functions across scripts - The code has distinct logical sections (config, data, logic, CLI) - You plan to distribute it (pip install)

issues.original: There are two dominant project structures in the Python ecosystem. ### Flat Layout The package directory sits at the project root. This is simpler and used by many projects including Flask and Requests. ### src Layout The package directory is inside [CODE]. This layout is recommended by the Python Packaging Authority (PyPA) and has one critical advantage: **it forces you to install your package before testing it.** This catches packaging errors (missing files, broken imports) before you ship.
issues.fixed: There are two dominant project structures in the Python ecosystem. ### Flat Layout The package directory sits at the project root. This is simpler and used by many projects, including Flask and Requests. ### src Layout The package directory is inside [CODE]. This layout is recommended by the Python Packaging Authority (PyPA) and has one critical advantage: **it forces you to install your package before testing it.** This catches packaging errors (missing files, broken imports) before you ship.

issues.original: With the flat layout, [CODE] resolves to the local directory even if the package is not properly installable. With src layout, Python cannot find [CODE] unless you run [CODE] first. This is a feature, not a bug.
issues.fixed: With the flat layout, [CODE] resolves to the local directory even if the package is not properly installable. With the src layout, Python cannot find [CODE] unless you run [CODE] first. This is a feature, not a bug.

issues.original: **Use src layout for libraries** you plan to publish. **Use flat layout for applications** where you control the deployment environment. When in doubt, use src layout.
issues.fixed: **Use src layout for libraries** you plan to publish. **Use flat layout for applications** where you control the deployment environment. When in doubt, use src layout.

issues.original: A directory becomes a Python package when it contains [CODE]. This file can be empty or contain initialization code. ### What [CODE] Does 1. **Marks a directory as a package** so Python can import from it 2. **Runs on import** — code in [CODE] executes when someone does [CODE] 3. **Controls the public API** via [CODE]
issues.fixed: A directory becomes a Python package when it contains [CODE]. This file can be empty or contain initialization code. ### What [CODE] Does 1. **Marks a directory as a package** so Python can import from it 2. **Runs on import** — code in [CODE] executes when someone imports it 3. **Controls the public API** via [CODE]

issues.original: Solutions: 1. **Move shared constants to a separate module** ([CODE] or [CODE]) 2. **Import inside functions** instead of at module level (delays the import) 3. **Restructure** — if two modules are tightly coupled, maybe they should be one module

issues.original: Your script downloads 100 files one at a time. Each download takes 2 seconds, mostly waiting for the server to respond. Total time: 200 seconds. But your CPU is idle for 99% of that time. You're paying for network latency and wasting compute. Concurrency fixes this.
issues.fixed: Your script downloads 100 files one at a time. Each download takes 2 seconds, mostly waiting for the server to respond. Total time: 200 seconds. Your CPU is idle for 99% of that time, wasting compute and money on network latency. Concurrency can fix this.

issues.original: Python has three concurrency models, each designed for different problems. Choose the wrong one, and your code will be either slow or full of race conditions. This article explains when to use each.
issues.fixed: Python has three concurrency models, each designed for different problems. Choosing the wrong one can make your code slow or full of race conditions. This article explains when to use each.

issues.original: The Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) is a mutex that protects access to Python objects. Only one thread can execute Python bytecode at a time, even on a multi-core machine.
issues.fixed: The Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) is a mutex that protects access to Python objects. Only one thread can execute Python bytecode at a time, even on a multi-core machine.

issues.original: Wait, the GIL does not prevent this? Correct. [CODE] compiles to multiple bytecodes (LOAD, ADD, STORE), and the GIL can release between them. The GIL protects interpreter internals, not your application logic.
issues.fixed: Wait, the GIL doesn't prevent this? Correct. [CODE] compiles to multiple bytecodes (LOAD, ADD, STORE), and the GIL can release between them. The GIL protects interpreter internals, not your application logic.

issues.original: Threads share the same memory space and are lightweight. The GIL releases during I/O operations, making threads effective for network calls, file operations, and database queries.
issues.fixed: Threads share the same memory space and are lightweight. The GIL releases during I/O operations, making them effective for network calls, file operations, and database queries.

issues.original: Each process has its own Python interpreter and GIL, enabling true parallelism on multiple CPU cores.
issues.fixed: Each process has its own Python interpreter and GIL, enabling true parallelism on multiple CPU cores.

issues.original: Don't use multiprocessing for small tasks. The serialization and process startup overhead can make it slower than sequential execution.
issues.fixed: Don't use multiprocessing for small tasks. The serialization and process startup overhead can make it slower than sequential execution.

issues.original: Note the [CODE] guard. This is required on macOS and Windows because multiprocessing uses [CODE] to create new processes, which re-imports the module.
issues.fixed: Note the [CODE] guard. This is required on macOS and Windows because multiprocessing uses [CODE] to create new processes, which re-imports the module.

issues.original: asyncio uses a single thread with an event loop. Functions voluntarily give up control at [CODE] points, allowing other tasks to run. No threads, no locks, no GIL worries.
issues.fixed: asyncio uses a single thread with an event loop. Functions voluntarily give up control at [CODE] points, allowing other tasks to run. No threads, no locks, and no GIL worries.

issues.original: Both threaded and async complete in about 1 second (the server delay). Async uses fewer system resources because there are no thread stacks or context switches.
issues.fixed: Both threaded and async complete in about 1 second (the server delay). Async uses fewer system resources because it doesn't have thread stacks or context switches.

issues.original: The second sentence is the important one. Performance work isn't about making everything fast; it's about finding the 3% that matters and making that fast.
issues.fixed: The second sentence is the key. Performance work isn't about making everything fast; it's about finding the 3% that matters and making that fast.

issues.original: This article is about finding that 3%. You'll learn to profile first, optimize second, and measure the impact of every change.
issues.fixed: This article is about finding that 3%. You'll learn to profile first, optimize second, and measure the impact of each change.

issues.original: For micro-benchmarks of small code snippets.
issues.fixed: For micro-benchmarks of small code snippets.

issues.original: List comprehension wins, but the difference is often small enough that readability should take precedence over micro-optimization.
issues.fixed: List comprehension wins, but the difference is often small enough that readability should take precedence over micro-optimization.

issues.original: cProfile tracks every function call, including how many times it was called and how long it took.
issues.fixed: cProfile tracks every function call, including how many times it was called and how long it took.

issues.original: Sort options.
issues.fixed: Sort options.

issues.original: snakeviz opens an interactive visualization with a sunburst chart of function call times, making the functions that take the most time immediately visible.
issues.fixed: snakeviz opens an interactive visualization with a sunburst chart of function call times, making the slowest functions immediately visible.

issues.original: cProfile tells you which functions are slow. [CODE] tells you which lines within those functions are slow.
issues.fixed: cProfile tells you which functions are slow. [CODE] tells you which lines within those functions are slow.

issues.original: Line 15 (the [CODE] with dict creation) takes 65% of the time. That is the optimization target.
issues.fixed: Line 15 (the [CODE] with dict creation) takes 65% of the time. This is the optimization target.

issues.original: Memoization stores the results of expensive function calls and returns the cached result when the same inputs occur again.
issues.fixed: Memoization stores the results of expensive function calls and returns the cached result when the same inputs occur again.

issues.original: For unlimited cache.
issues.fixed: For unlimited cache.

issues.original: **Warning:** [CODE] stores results in memory. For functions that return large objects or are called with many different arguments, the cache can consume significant memory. Set [CODE] to limit it.
issues.fixed: **Warning:** [CODE] stores results in memory. For functions that return large objects or are called with many different arguments, the cache can consume significant memory. Set [CODE] to limit it.

issues.original: Python loops are slow because each iteration involves type checking, reference counting, and bytecode interpretation. NumPy pushes the loop into optimized C code.
issues.fixed: Python loops are slow because each iteration involves type checking, reference counting, and bytecode interpretation. NumPy pushes the loop into optimized C code.

issues.original: Generators produce values one at a time instead of creating the entire collection in memory.
issues.fixed: Generators produce values one at a time instead of creating the entire collection in memory.

issues.original: By default, Python objects store attributes in a [CODE] dictionary. [CODE] replaces this with a fixed-size array.
issues.fixed: By default, Python objects store attributes in a [CODE] dictionary. [CODE] replaces this with a fixed-size array.

issues.original: Roughly 3x memory savings. Use [CODE] when you create millions of instances of the same class (data points, graph nodes, ORM rows).
issues.fixed: Roughly 3x memory savings. Use [CODE] when you create millions of instances of the same class (data points, graph nodes, ORM rows).

issues.original: **Trade-off:** [CODE] objects cannot have arbitrary attributes added dynamically, and they do not support multiple inheritance with other [CODE] classes easily.
issues.fixed: **Trade-off:** [CODE] objects cannot have arbitrary attributes added dynamically, and they do not support multiple inheritance with other [CODE] classes easily.

issues.original: Cython compiles Python-like code to C and can speed up CPU-bound code by 10-100x.
issues.fixed: Cython compiles Python-like code to C and can speed up CPU-bound code by 10-100x.

issues.original: Cython is a big topic. For most applications, the optimization techniques earlier in this article (vectorization, caching, generators) are sufficient. Use Cython when you've verified with profiling that a specific hot loop is the bottleneck and NumPy cannot help because the computation is not easily vectorizable.
issues.fixed: Cython is a big topic. For most applications, the optimization techniques earlier in this article (vectorization, caching, generators) are sufficient. Use Cython when you've verified with profiling that a specific hot loop is the bottleneck and NumPy cannot help because the computation is not easily vectorizable.

issues.original: Before optimizing, ask these questions in order.
issues.fixed: Before optimizing, ask these questions in order.

issues.original: | Step | Question | Action |
issues.fixed: | Step | Question | Action |

issues.original: | 1 | Is it actually slow? | Measure with [CODE] |
issues.fixed: | 1 | Is it actually slow? | Measure with [CODE] |

issues.original: | 2 | Where is it slow? | Profile with cProfile |
issues.fixed: | 2 | Where is it slow? | Profile with cProfile |

issues.original: | 3 | Which lines are slow? | Profile with line_profiler |
issues.fixed: | 3 | Which lines are slow? | Profile with line_profiler |

issues.original: | 4 | Is it I/O-bound or CPU-bound? | Check if CPU usage is high or low during the slow part. |
issues.fixed: | 4 | Is it I/O-bound or CPU-bound? | Check if CPU usage is high or low during the slow part. |

issues.original: | 5 | Can I use a better algorithm? | O(n) vs O(n^2) matters more than any micro-optimization |
issues.fixed: | 5 | Can I use a better algorithm? | O(n) vs O(n^2) matters more than any micro-optimization |

issues.original: | 6 | Can I cache results? | [CODE] for repeated computations |
issues.fixed: | 6 | Can I cache results? | [CODE] for repeated computations |

issues.original: | 7 | Can I vectorize? | NumPy for numerical array operations |
issues.fixed: | 7 | Can I vectorize? | NumPy for numerical array operations |

issues.original: | 8 | Can I use generators? | Lazy evaluation for memory-bound problems |
issues.fixed: | 8 | Can I use generators? | Lazy evaluation for memory-bound problems |

issues.original: | 9 | Can I use concurrency? | Threading for I/O, multiprocessing for CPU. |
issues.fixed: | 9 | Can I use concurrency? | Threading for I/O, multiprocessing for CPU. |

issues.original: | 10 | Can I use C extensions? | Cython, pybind11 (last resort). |
issues.fixed: | 10 | Can I use C extensions? | Cython, pybind11 (last resort). |

issues.original: | Technique | Typical Speedup | Effort | Best For |
issues.fixed: | Technique | Typical Speedup | Effort | Best For |

issues.original: | Better algorithm | 10-1000x | Medium | O(n^2) -> O(n log n) |
issues.fixed: | Better algorithm | 10-1000x | Medium | O(n^2) -> O(n log n) |

issues.original: | NumPy vectorization | 10-200x | Low | Numerical array operations |
issues.fixed: | NumPy vectorization | 10-200x | Low | Numerical array operations |

issues.original: | Caching | 2-1000x | Low | Repeated expensive calls |
issues.fixed: | Caching | 2-1000x | Low | Repeated expensive calls |

issues.original: | asyncio/threading | 2-50x | Medium | I
issues.fixed: | asyncio/threading | 2-50x | Medium | I
